paragraphs: Akane is a Japanese apple developed at the Morioka research station from a cross of Jonathan and Worcester Pearmain. It is also known as Tokyo Rose, Tohoku No.3 and Prime Red, and was introduced to the United States in the late 1930s.

It is a bright-red, flattish to heart-shaped apple with crisp white flesh that does not brown quickly. The flavour is a lively sweet-sharp balance carrying the zesty character of Jonathan, often with strawberry and floral notes. It is one of the better early-season apples and keeps its shape when cooked.

paragraphs: Anna is a low-chill apple bred by Abba Stein at the Ein Shemer kibbutz in Israel in 1959, descended from Golden Delicious and developed specifically for warm climates. Its very low chill requirement, far below what most apples need, makes it a popular choice for subtropical Australian gardens.

It is very early ripening and crops heavily, with crisp creamy flesh, a sweet balanced flavour, and green to greenish-yellow skin flushed with red. Anna needs cross-pollination and is commonly grown alongside Dorsett Golden or Ein Shemer.

paragraphs: Bolero is one of the Ballerina columnar apples, originally named Tuscan. It was bred at East Malling Research Station in Kent from a Wijcik McIntosh columnar mutation crossed with Greensleeves. The Ballerina habit is genetic, not the result of pruning: the tree forms a single upright stem with minimal branching, carrying its fruit on very short spurs close to the trunk. That makes it narrow and well suited to small gardens, courtyards and patio pots, needing little to no pruning.
It is a dessert apple, eaten fresh. The flesh is described as cream coloured, moderately crisp and juicy with a sweet-sharp flavour, similar to its Greensleeves parent.

paragraphs: Flamenco is one of the Ballerina columnar apples, developed at East Malling Research Station in Kent. Its breeding traces back to a Wijcik McIntosh columnar mutation, and the columnar habit is a genetic trait rather than a product of pruning. The tree grows as a single upright stem with very little side branching, carrying its fruit on short spurs along the central trunk. This gives a tall, very narrow tree that is well suited to small gardens and patio containers and needs minimal pruning.
It is grown mainly as a fresh-eating dessert apple, with crisp white flesh, and is one of the later-maturing apples in the Ballerina range.

paragraphs: Polka is one of the Ballerina range of columnar apples, raised in 1976 in Ken Tobutt's breeding programme at East Malling Research Station in Kent by crossing Golden Delicious with the naturally columnar Wijcik McIntosh. The variety was first named Trajan and is sold under the Polka name. Like the rest of the group it grows as a slim upright pole, carrying its fruit on short spurs along the main trunk rather than on spreading branches, so it stays narrow, needs very little pruning, and suits small gardens, courtyards and large pots.

The apples are medium sized, greenish yellow with a red blush on the sun exposed side. The white flesh is firm, crisp and juicy, sweet with a good sweet-tart balance. It is a late season dessert variety, and it is self-sterile, so it needs a compatible pollination partner nearby to set a good crop.

paragraphs: Waltz belongs to the Ballerina range of columnar apples bred at East Malling Research Station in Kent, created in 1976 by pollinating the naturally columnar Wijcik Spur McIntosh with Golden Delicious. It was first named Telamon and is marketed as Waltz, and in some markets Ultraspire. The tree forms a narrow upright column with fruiting spurs close to the main stem, so it needs little pruning and makes a tidy, decorative choice for small gardens and containers.

The fruit is medium to large and round to round-conic, green ripening to golden yellow with a substantial red blush. The cream coloured flesh is medium grained, crisp and juicy, moderately sweet with a degree of tartness. It is a dessert apple, and only partly self fertile, so it crops best with another compatible apple flowering nearby.

paragraphs: Beauty Of Bath is one of the classic early-season English dessert apples, raised at Bailbrook near Bath, Somerset, and introduced by the nurseryman George Cooling around 1864. In Victorian times it was prized as one of the earliest apples to ripen, well before the main-season varieties, which made it a commercial favourite of its day.

The fruit is small and flattish, with a bright red flush over yellow, and a proportion of fruits show pink staining in the white flesh. The flavour starts sharp and turns sweeter and softer as it ripens. It is a true summer apple: it bruises easily, drops when not fully ripe and does not keep, so it is best eaten straight from the tree.

paragraphs: Belle Cacheuse is the Australian rendering of the French name Belle Cauchoise, a traditional cider apple of Normandy whose name refers to the Pays de Caux district. The Australian spelling arose during importation, and the NSW Department of Primary Industries confirmed it as one of several French cider varieties that had been grown in Australia for years without being recognised as such.

It is a notably large, flattish apple with green skin striped red on the sunny side, unusual among dedicated cider apples in being useful for eating and cooking as well as juice. Because it is on the sweet side rather than strongly tannic, it is best added to a blend alongside sharp and bitter varieties. The tree is vigorous and tends to biennial cropping.

paragraphs: Bonza is an Australian apple that arose as a chance seedling in the late 1950s, found by Ben Atkinson in his orchard in the Batlow area of the Snowy Mountains region of New South Wales. Unusually for an Australian apple, it is a relatively high-chill variety, reflecting its cool mountain origins.

The fruit is medium to large and flat-conic, ripening to cream with a crimson blush over much of the skin. The flesh is white, firm, crisp and juicy with a sweet flavour, and it keeps for several months in cold storage. It is grown mainly as a fresh-eating apple.

paragraphs: Braeburn is a New Zealand apple, discovered as a chance seedling in 1952 near Motueka and named after the Braeburn orchard where it was first grown commercially. Long assumed to be linked to Granny Smith or Lady Hamilton, a 2020 parentage study identified its parents as Delicious and Sturmer Pippin. It became one of the first widely exported New Zealand apples and is now grown in apple regions worldwide.

The fruit is firm and crisp with red-orange streaks over a green-gold base, and a well-balanced sweet-tart, aromatic flavour rather than a one-note sweetness. It is a late-season variety harvested into autumn, and it stores exceptionally well, keeping for months in controlled-atmosphere storage. The firm flesh holds its shape in cooking, making it useful for tarts and baking as well as for eating fresh.

paragraphs: Bramley's Seedling is widely regarded as the definitive English cooking apple. It traces to a single tree grown from pips planted by Mary Ann Brailsford in her garden at Southwell, Nottinghamshire, in 1809. The cottage and tree later passed to the butcher Matthew Bramley, and in the 1850s a local nurseryman, Henry Merryweather, took cuttings to propagate it on the condition the apples carried Bramley's name. Every Bramley today descends from that original tree.

Bramley's is a triploid, which means it cannot pollinate other apples and needs to be grown near two compatible pollinating varieties. The fruit is large and strongly acidic, so it is almost always eaten cooked rather than fresh. When baked it breaks down to a light, golden, fluffy puree, which is why it is prized for pies, crumbles and sauces.

paragraphs: Brown Snout is a 19th century bittersweet cider apple from Herefordshire, discovered around 1850 as a chance seedling on Mr Dent's farm at Yarkhill and later propagated widely by the H. P. Bulmer company of Hereford. Its name comes from the heavy patch of brown russet around the calyx end of the fruit, which resembles a snout.
It is a late-flowering variety with relatively high tannins and low malic acid, giving a mild bittersweet, dark cider with soft tannins. It can stand alone or be blended for added acidity.

paragraphs: Bulmer's Norman is a French-origin bittersweet cider apple found growing in Normandy and brought to England by H. P. Bulmer & Co. of Hereford in the early 1890s. On its own it makes a mild bittersweet cider, with a golden, sweet, astringent juice that ferments quickly.

It is grown for its hard, bitter tannin, which adds body and texture in blends. The tree is large, vigorous and spreading, and a dependable but biennial cropper. It is also valued as a sturdy rootstock and interstem onto which other varieties are grafted.

paragraphs: Chataingier is the Australian rendering of the French cider apple Chataignier, whose name was slightly corrupted during importation. The NSW Department of Primary Industries lists it under the original French spelling among cider varieties confirmed to be growing in Australia. It is a very old variety, said to have originated in Normandy, France, perhaps as far back as the 1200s.

The fruit is medium to large and round-flattened, with thin glossy skin that is greenish-yellow washed and broken-striped with dull dark red. The flesh is white and breaking, fairly sweet and subacid with a tannic note, and it is used for cider.

paragraphs: Improved Foxwhelp belongs to the Foxwhelp family of cider apples, a very old group from the west Midlands of England (Herefordshire or Gloucestershire) first recorded in the 17th century. Foxwhelps are classed as bittersharp, combining high malic acid with high tannin, which makes a powerful, very sharp cider traditionally used to add strength and kick to blends. The original Foxwhelp was so prone to producing branch sports that it spawned a string of selections, and the improved or rejuvenated Foxwhelps were 19th century attempts to revive the failing old variety by grafting its scions onto its own seedlings.
Improved Foxwhelp has skin deeply striped crimson and yellow, with tender yellow flesh tinged red and a rough, acid flavour. It is an early-ripening cider apple.

paragraphs: Kingston Black is the most famous of the English bittersharp cider apples, carrying high levels of both tannin and acid in the one fruit. It arose in the early 1800s around the parish of Kingston St Mary near Taunton in Somerset, where locals knew it simply as the black apple for its dark red, almost black skin over a yellow ground.

Its fame rests on balance. Tannin, acid and sugar sit in proportions that let it carry a single varietal cider on its own, something very few cider apples manage, and it is widely treated as the benchmark vintage variety, in Australia as much as in England. The tree is less obliging than the fruit, slow to come into bearing, inclined to biennial cropping, and susceptible to scab and canker.

paragraphs: Cimetiere de Blangy is a Norman cider apple with a literal graveyard origin. The original tree grew in the cemetery at Blangy-le-Chateau, a village in the Pays d'Auge near Lisieux, and the variety simply took the cemetery's name. It is classed douce amere, the French equivalent of a bittersweet, and was among the varieties officially approved in France for planting in the Calvados producing region, the apple brandy heartland of Normandy.

In Australia it is a rarity that arrived under the wrong name. Early material circulated as Cimetiere du Pays before the misidentification was untangled, and that synonym still trails the variety in reference lists. Its appeal for collectors is the documented Calvados pedigree, a direct link to the traditional cider orchards of the Pays d'Auge.

paragraphs: Cornish Aromatic is a heritage dessert apple from Cornwall, England, first recorded in 1813 when Sir Christopher Hawkins brought it to notice by sending fruit to the London Horticultural Society, though it is thought to be far older. It became a popular apple of country estates and cottage gardens.

The fruit is handsome and bright red with patches of grey-brown russet and darker red striping. The firm, fine-textured flesh has a crisp, nutty, aromatic and lightly spicy flavour. It is a late-season apple, picked in mid-autumn and good for eating through the winter.

paragraphs: Cox's Orange Pippin was first grown around 1825 to 1830 at Colnbrook in Buckinghamshire, England, by Richard Cox, a retired brewer turned horticulturist. It became England's leading dessert apple and by the early 2000s accounted for more than half the UK dessert apple acreage. DNA analysis points to Margil as a parent.

The medium-sized fruit is orange-red over a yellow background, mottled with carmine. The yellow-white flesh is fine-grained, crisp, very juicy and highly aromatic, with a sprightly subacid flavour carrying hints of cherry and anise that softens with age. It is celebrated as a richly flavoured eating apple.

paragraphs: Crimson Crisp, originally trialled as Co-op 39, came from the cooperative apple breeding program run by the Indiana (Purdue), Illinois and New Jersey (Rutgers) experiment stations. The original seedling traces back to a 1971 cross at Cream Ridge, New Jersey, and carries scab resistance bred in from the crabapple Malus floribunda. The trade name highlights its red colour and crisp flesh.

It is a midseason dessert apple with an attractive crimson-red skin and yellow flesh. The texture is very crisp and breaking, and the flavour is rich and subacid. The fruit holds on the tree without dropping when mature, and keeps well in storage.

paragraphs: Crofton Red is an old heritage apple kept in Australian collections and grown as a dessert (fresh eating) variety. Accounts of its origin differ between sources, so its exact background is uncertain, but it has long been valued as a heritage tree. The fruit is small to medium and round, striped and flushed red over a greenish-yellow base, which gives it an attractive, old-fashioned look.

The flesh is white, crisp and sweet, with a rich, lightly spicy, sweet-subacid character. It is a late-season apple, ripening in autumn (around March to April in Australia), and is grown mainly as a dessert apple for fresh eating.

paragraphs: Cumulus is a columnar (pillar form) apple, one of a series bred at the Research and Breeding Institute of Pomology in Holovousy, Czech Republic. Like other columnar apples it descends from the Wijcik McIntosh mutation, so it grows as a narrow upright single stem with very short fruiting spurs and almost no side branches. That habit makes it suited to pots, courtyards and tight spaces rather than a spreading orchard tree, and it needs little structural pruning to keep its slim shape.

Cumulus carries genetic resistance to apple scab, though it can be susceptible to powdery mildew to varying degrees. In Australian supply it is sold as a late season red eating apple, and being self sterile it needs a compatible pollinator nearby such as the related columnar varieties Herald or Harmony.

paragraphs: Dabinett is a traditional English bittersweet cider apple from Somerset, found by William Dabinett growing as a wilding in a hedge at Middle Lambrook, South Petherton, around the early 1900s. One probable parent was the late bittersweet variety Chisel Jersey.

It is prized as a vintage cider variety, capable of making a soft, full-bodied, high quality single varietal cider. As a bittersweet it carries high tannin with low acidity, giving a sweet, astringent juice. The tree is a weak, spreading grower and fruit ripens late, usually November in Britain.

paragraphs: Democrat is a Tasmanian heritage apple found growing about 1900 in the orchard of J. Duffy near Hobart, and originally known as Duffy's Seedling. It was briefly renamed Tasma in 1913 to avoid confusion with an American apple, though that name did not endure. It is possibly a seedling of Hoover.

The fruit is medium to large and conic, maturing to a dull dark red over a greenish-yellow base, with very firm, crisp, juicy flesh that is sweet and mild. Democrat is best known for ripening very late and then keeping for many months in cold storage, which made it a mainstay of the Tasmanian export trade for decades.

paragraphs: Dorsett Golden is a low-chill apple that originated in the Bahamas around 1953 as a seedling at the home of Mrs Irene Dorsett in Nassau. Its very low chill requirement makes it one of the best apples for warm, subtropical parts of Australia where standard apples will not fruit.

The fruit is yellow to yellow-green, often with a pink blush, with firm white flesh and a sweet, lightly aromatic flavour. It is largely self-fertile but crops better with a pollinator, and pairs well with the similarly low-chill Anna apple, which flowers at the same time.

paragraphs: Ein Shemer is an Israeli apple raised by Abba Stein at the Ein Shemer kibbutz, a cross of Zabidani and Golden Delicious introduced in the 1960s. It is one of the standout low-chill apples for warm and subtropical districts, listed by University of California horticulturists among the varieties that fruit reliably where winter cold is minimal. That makes it a sensible pick for milder Australian gardens where most apples struggle to set fruit.

The fruit is medium to large and Golden Delicious in type, golden-yellow at maturity (sometimes with a light blush on the sunny side), with juicy, sweet-sharp pale flesh. It is partially self-fertile, so it can crop on its own but tends to do better with a pollinator nearby. It is an early, heavy bearer.

paragraphs: Empire is a McIntosh-type apple from Cornell University's New York State Agricultural Experiment Station at Geneva. It grew from a seed sown in 1945 by Lester C. Anderson and was released in 1966, named for the Empire State of New York. Its parents are McIntosh and Red Delicious.

The fruit combines the crisp, tangy McIntosh character with the sweetness and deep crimson colour of Red Delicious, over creamy white flesh. It is crisp, juicy and sweet-tart, slower to brown than most, and a good all-purpose apple. It is harvested in early autumn and stores well.

paragraphs: Fenouillet Gris is an old French dessert apple that originated in Anjou and was first described in 1608. Its defining feature is flavour. The name comes from fenouil (fennel), and the fruit carries a distinct, spicy aniseed taste that sets it apart from almost every other apple. It collected a long list of synonyms over the centuries, among them Pomme d'Anis and, in England, Caraway Russet.

The fruit is small to medium and heavily russeted, with a dull yellow base covered in netted reddish-brown and light russet. The flesh is fine, white, crisp and on the dry side, sweet with that signature anise note. Picked around mid October, it works as a fresh eating apple and is also used for cider and dried apple rings.

paragraphs: Five Crown Pippin is an old English apple, also known as London Pippin, with origins that are somewhat confused but documented from the 1500s. It was being sold in Norfolk in the 1700s as a genuine local apple. Its most recognisable feature is its shape: the fruit is prominently ribbed and crowned at the eye, giving the distinctive five-crowned outline that names it.

It is a medium green apple with no russet, becoming more yellow as it matures. The flesh is fairly acidic, sometimes with a nutty character, and the apple keeps its shape well when cooked, making it a useful culinary variety. It is also a long keeper, harvested in mid-autumn and usable through the winter months.

paragraphs: Flamenco is one of the Ballerina columnar apples, developed at East Malling Research Station in Kent. Its breeding traces back to a Wijcik McIntosh columnar mutation, and the columnar habit is a genetic trait rather than a product of pruning. The tree grows as a single upright stem with very little side branching, carrying its fruit on short spurs along the central trunk. This gives a tall, very narrow tree that is well suited to small gardens and patio containers and needs minimal pruning.
It is grown mainly as a fresh-eating dessert apple, with crisp white flesh, and is one of the later-maturing apples in the Ballerina range.

paragraphs: Forfar Pippin is an old dual-purpose apple whose exact origin is uncertain. It is often linked to the Dutch Mignonne (also called Albury Park Nonesuch), thought to have come from the Netherlands and brought to Britain in 1771, though it is also recorded by Australian heritage nurseries as a Scottish apple. Whatever its roots, it earned its reputation in the kitchen as much as on the table.

It is best known as the traditional apple for Apple Charlotte, holding its shape well when cooked and making a flavourful, sweet-tart sauce. The fruit is medium to large and round-conic, often a little irregular and ribbed, with a yellow base, pale reddish streaks and patches of russet. The firm, crisp flesh is juicy, aromatic and sweet, so it doubles as a dessert apple as well as a cooker.

paragraphs: Frequin Rouge is a traditional French cider apple associated with Normandy, where it is one of the named varieties permitted under the Pays d'Auge appellation for cider and Calvados. In the French classification it falls among the bitter to bittersweet group, prized for the depth and tannin it brings to a blend. It is regarded as a vintage variety, and its sometimes-used Australian name is Frequin Rouge Amer, the word amer meaning bitter.

The fruit is small to medium, yellow or pale green with broken red stripes, and the flesh is crisp and juicy with very high tannin, far too astringent to eat fresh. Like many old cider varieties it tends to biennial bearing, cropping heavily in alternate years.

paragraphs: Freyberg is a New Zealand dessert apple raised by J.H. Kidd at Greytown in the Wairarapa, from a cross of Golden Delicious and Cox's Orange Pippin. Bred in the 1930s and released in 1959, it was named after Lord Bernard Freyberg, the Governor-General of New Zealand. In appearance it strongly resembles a small Golden Delicious, with golden-green skin and creamy-white, crisp flesh.

Its appeal is in the flavour, which combines the sweetness of Golden Delicious with a Cox-like aromatic character, sometimes described as having a distinct pear-like note. Despite this quality it never became a major commercial apple, partly because of its close resemblance to Golden Delicious, so it remains a variety grown mostly by enthusiasts.

paragraphs: Fuji was developed by growers at the Tohoku Research Station in Fujisaki, Aomori, Japan, from a cross of the American varieties Ralls Janet and Red Delicious. It was named and brought to market in 1962, with its name taken from the town of Fujisaki where it was bred.

The fruit is prized for dense flesh that is notably sweet, crisp and very juicy, with cream-coloured flesh and a flavour that can carry hints of honey and pear. It is a late-season apple that stores exceptionally well, staying fresh for many months in refrigerated storage, which is part of why it became a global supermarket staple.

paragraphs: Gala was raised by orchardist J.H. Kidd at Greytown, New Zealand in the 1930s, a cross of Kidd's Orange Red with Golden Delicious. It was selected in 1939 and introduced commercially around 1960, going on to become one of the most widely grown apples in the world.

The fruit is sweet and mild with a crisp but not hard texture and a fragrant, aromatic flavour. Its skin is non-uniform, usually striped or mottled with an overall orange to reddish colour. It is mainly an eating apple and ripens in late summer to autumn.

paragraphs: Geeveston Fanny is a Tasmanian heritage apple thought to have originated at Geeveston in the Huon Valley, where the oldest known tree grew in James Evans's orchard in 1880. It was a popular commercial variety in Tasmania before modern supermarket apples pushed it aside.

The medium fruit has a pale lemon and light green base, with much of the skin flushed pale red and marked with darker stripes. The flesh is crisp with a subacid, aromatic flavour, and it is a late-season dessert apple.

paragraphs: Golden Delicious arose as a chance seedling on the Mullins family farm in Clay County, West Virginia, and was discovered in the early twentieth century. Stark Brothers Nurseries bought the original tree and its propagation rights and began marketing it alongside Red Delicious in 1914. It is the official state fruit of West Virginia.

The fruit has thin yellowish-green skin and very sweet, crisp, juicy flesh, often with a honeyed, almost floral note. It is versatile for fresh eating, salads, sauce and baking, where it holds together well. The skin bruises and shrivels easily, so it needs gentle handling. It is also a parent of later cultivars including Gala.

paragraphs: Golden Harvey, also known as the Brandy apple, is one of the older English varieties, believed to have originated in Herefordshire around the 1600s. It is a small dessert apple whose skin is almost entirely covered in rough golden russet. The name refers as much to its notably yellow flesh as to the russeted skin, and the flesh is crisp and firm.

Although it can be eaten fresh, Golden Harvey is best known as a cider apple. Its juice is intensely sweet and strong, producing a cider of unusual potency, which earned it the alternative name Brandy. Popular in Victorian times, it later fell out of favour and is now found mainly in specialist heritage orchards. It is also valued as a long keeper, holding its quality well into the following year.

paragraphs: Granny Smith is an Australian apple discovered in 1868 by Maria Ann Smith at Eastwood, New South Wales. It grew as a chance seedling near a creek where she had discarded the remains of French crab-apples grown in Tasmania, and is thought to be a cross of the domestic apple with a wild apple species.

The fruit has bright green skin, hard firm flesh, and a tart, sour, acidic flavour. It is valued for both cooking (pies, crumbles) and fresh eating, and its excellent shelf life made it a major export variety worldwide.

paragraphs: Gravenstein is an old triploid apple linked to Graasten Palace in southern Denmark, where it was recorded by 1669, though it likely originated earlier in Germany or Italy. Because it is triploid, it needs another apple to pollinate it and is itself a poor pollinator. In 2005 the Danish food minister declared it the national apple of Denmark.

The fruit has waxy yellow-green skin with crimson spots and reddish lines, and juicy, light yellow flesh with a lively, tart flavour. It is prized for cooking, especially applesauce and cider, but does not keep well, so it is mostly available only in season.

paragraphs: Grimes Golden is a prized American heirloom apple, found as a chance seedling in Brooke County, in what is now West Virginia, in the early 1800s on land farmed by Thomas Grimes. It is widely regarded as a likely parent of the famous Golden Delicious, which was discovered in the same part of West Virginia and went on to eclipse it in commercial orchards.

The apple is golden-yellow with firm, fine-grained, yellowish flesh and a distinctive rich, spicy, aromatic and slightly sub-acid flavour that sets it apart from blander modern yellow apples. It serves as both a dessert and a culinary apple. It can be prone to bitter pit, a physiological spotting disorder, under some growing conditions.

paragraphs: Herald is a columnar apple from the series bred at the Research and Breeding Institute of Pomology in Holovousy, Czech Republic. As with other columnar (pillar) apples it traces back to the Wijcik McIntosh mutation and grows as a slim, upright single stem that fruits on short spurs close to the trunk, so it stays narrow without training. That makes it a practical choice for tubs, balconies and small gardens where a normal apple tree would not fit.

A notable point of difference for Herald is its disease package: it possesses combined resistance to both apple scab and powdery mildew, which is stronger than several of its sibling cultivars. In Australia it is offered as a red skinned eating apple, and like all columnar apples it is not self fertile, so it needs another compatible apple flowering at the same time to set a crop.

paragraphs: Hubbardston Nonsuch is an American heritage dessert apple that originated in the town of Hubbardston, Massachusetts, and was first recorded in 1832. The name Nonsuch (a form of nonpareil, meaning without equal) was attached to several prized apples of the period. It was a popular market apple in the New England and New York region during the nineteenth century, and Hubbardston still features apples on its town seal.

The fruit has firm, fine-textured white flesh with a sweet, subacid flavour. It carries a green-yellow ground colour, sometimes with brown streaking, on a conical fruit. Once a favourite around Boston, it is now a rare variety found mainly in specialist orchards and home gardens.

paragraphs: Improved Foxwhelp belongs to the Foxwhelp family of cider apples, a very old group from the west Midlands of England (Herefordshire or Gloucestershire) first recorded in the 17th century. Foxwhelps are classed as bittersharp, combining high malic acid with high tannin, which makes a powerful, very sharp cider traditionally used to add strength and kick to blends. The original Foxwhelp was so prone to producing branch sports that it spawned a string of selections, and the improved or rejuvenated Foxwhelps were 19th century attempts to revive the failing old variety by grafting its scions onto its own seedlings.
Improved Foxwhelp has skin deeply striped crimson and yellow, with tender yellow flesh tinged red and a rough, acid flavour. It is an early-ripening cider apple.

paragraphs: The apple known as Isaac Newton's Tree is the variety Flower of Kent, an old green cooking apple. Its fame comes from its link to Sir Isaac Newton, who according to the well-known story watched an apple fall from a tree of this variety at his home, Woolsthorpe Manor, and was prompted to think about gravity. Most surviving Flower of Kent trees are said to descend from that historic tree.

Flower of Kent itself is a pear-shaped, mealy, sub-acid culinary apple, considered of generally poor eating quality by modern standards. It has largely vanished from commercial cultivation and survives mainly as a heritage curiosity, propagated for its scientific and historical associations rather than its flavour.

paragraphs: James Grieve is a Scottish dessert apple, raised by James Grieve in Edinburgh and recorded by 1893. Its parentage is not certain but is usually attributed to Pott's Seedling or Cox's Orange Pippin. A reliable, regular cropper with good disease resistance, it is also valued as an effective pollinator for other apple varieties.

It is a juicy, refreshing apple with strong acidity when first picked that mellows and sweetens as it matures, making it dual-purpose for both cooking and eating. The flesh softens fairly quickly after harvest and the fruit bruises easily, so it travels and stores poorly. That unsuitability for supermarket handling is why it survives mainly as a garden and local-sale variety rather than a commercial one.

paragraphs: Jerseymac is an early McIntosh-style apple developed at the Rutgers Agricultural Experiment Station in New Jersey, released in 1971 from a cross of NJ-24 with July Red. Its appeal is timing: it ripens in mid to late summer, well ahead of true McIntosh, giving growers a McIntosh character early in the season.

The fruit is medium to large with a yellow-green base mostly washed in dark, bluish red, and greenish-white flesh that is soft, fine-grained and juicy. The flavour is a fragrant, well-balanced sweet-tart, a little sharper than McIntosh, which suits both fresh eating and cooking. Flavour peaks within about a week of picking and then fades, so it is best enjoyed soon after harvest.

paragraphs: Jonagold was bred in 1943 at the New York State Agricultural Experiment Station in Geneva, New York, and released in 1968. It is a cross of Golden Delicious and Jonathan, and its name is a portmanteau of its two parents.

The fruit is large with yellow-green skin streaked or blushed with crimson, and creamy-yellow flesh with a breaking, crisp texture. The flavour combines honeyed sweetness with a tart edge, more complex than either parent. It is a triploid with sterile pollen, so it cannot pollinate other apples and needs a separate pollinator variety nearby. It suits fresh eating, sauces and baking.

paragraphs: Jonathan is an American heirloom that grew as a seedling of Esopus Spitzenburg on Philip Rick's farm near Woodstock in Ulster County, New York, and was named in 1826. It became one of the most important nineteenth century commercial apples and is now more of a specialty variety.

The fruit is medium-sized with tough, smooth, deep-red skin over a paler background, and white to cream flesh that is crisp and juicy. The flavour is a lively sweet-tart balance with a hint of spice, leaning sharper early in the season and mellowing later. It suits fresh eating and cooking, and is a parent of Jonagold and other cultivars. It is notably susceptible to apple scab and fire blight.

paragraphs: King David is an American heritage apple found in 1893 as a chance seedling along a fence row on Ben Frost's farm in Washington County, Arkansas. It was introduced commercially in 1902 by Stark Brothers Nursery of Missouri. Its parentage is uncertain, with Jonathan generally accepted as one parent and Winesap or Arkansas Black as the other.

The fruit is deep, dark red, often almost covering the skin, over firm yellow flesh. The flavour is spicy, slightly sweet and tart with a cidery, Winesap-like character that is far spicier than its Jonathan parent. It suits fresh eating, pies, sauce and cider, and turns a rich yellow when cooked.

paragraphs: Lady Williams is a Western Australian heritage apple that began as a chance seedling at Boronia Farm in Donnybrook, thought to have come from a Granny Smith, with Rokewood as a likely pollen parent. It is named after Maud Williams, who recognised the tree's value and protected it, and it was introduced commercially in 1968.

The fruit matures very late in the season and is firm and crisp with good keeping qualities, which suited it to long storage and export. Lady Williams is best known as the pollen parent of Cripps Pink (sold as Pink Lady), as well as Cripps Red, after John Cripps crossed it with Golden Delicious in his WA breeding program.

paragraphs: Leprechaun is a naturally compact, dwarf form of Granny Smith. It arose as a branch sport on a Granny Smith tree, so it is a true dwarf in its own right (the tree itself is dwarfing) rather than a standard variety worked onto dwarfing rootstock. It is spur bearing and stays small with minimal pruning, which makes it well suited to pots and small gardens while still producing full sized, classic green Granny Smith apples.

The fruit keeps the Granny Smith character: bright green skin and crisp, tart, acidic flesh that is good both fresh and for cooking, and stores well. Granny Smith itself is an Australian apple, a chance seedling propagated by Maria Ann Smith near Sydney in the 1860s. Leprechaun is self sterile and needs a pollinator flowering at the same time; the dwarf Pink Lady, Pinkabelle, is a commonly recommended partner.

paragraphs: Magnus Summer Surprise is an Australian-bred apple from Bob Magnus, a Tasmanian heritage apple specialist, and is sold as a protected (PBR) variety. Its seed parent is the Huonville Crab, a tree Bob found growing on a roadside near Huonville, Tasmania; the pollen parent is unknown. It took roughly twenty years to develop.

The fruit is an early one, ripening in mid summer, which puts it among the earliest apples of the season. It is notable for deep pink flesh and a bright rose-red skin, with a sweet, slightly tart flavour carrying berry overtones. It eats well fresh and also makes a pink cider.

paragraphs: McIntosh is a Canadian heirloom discovered as a chance seedling by John McIntosh on his Dundela farm in Upper Canada, now Ontario, in 1811. Its parentage is uncertain, but it may have descended from the Fameuse, or Snow apple. The family began grafting and selling it in the 1830s, and it became the most popular apple in eastern Canada and the northeastern United States.

The fruit has red and green skin that is tender and easy to peel, with white, juicy flesh and an aromatic, sweet-tart flavour reminiscent of cider. It softens readily during storage and cooking, so it is handled gently. It is a hugely important breeding parent, behind cultivars such as Cortland, Spartan and Macoun, and it lent its name to the Macintosh computer.

paragraphs: Michelin is a French bittersweet cider apple raised by nurseryman Legrand of Yvetot in Normandy, first fruiting in 1872 and named after the French cider pomologist Henri Michelin. It was brought to Herefordshire by the Woolhope Naturalists Club in the mid-1880s and went on to become one of the most widely planted cider varieties in England.

It is valued more for reliability than for its juice on its own. Michelin is a dependable, heavy cropper that largely avoids biennial bearing, but the cider it yields, with soft tannins and a mild aromatic character, is fairly undistinguished alone and is best used for blending.

paragraphs: Monty's Surprise is a New Zealand heritage apple, rediscovered in April 2000 when a large old seedling tree was found in a remote part of the central North Island. The fruit is big, notably disease resistant, crisp and well flavoured, and the cultivar has become a focus of phytochemical and cancer-prevention research by the Heritage Food Crops Research Trust.

In a published feeding study, phytochemicals from Monty's Surprise apple were absorbed in humans and significantly raised plasma antioxidant response. In laboratory (in vitro) testing, the apple's metabolites inhibited the proliferation of lung and breast cancer cells, which has made it one of the most studied heritage apples for potential health benefits.

paragraphs: Mutsu, also sold as Crispin, was introduced from Aomori Prefecture, Japan, in 1949. It is a cross of Golden Delicious and the Japanese cultivar Indo, and is a triploid, which makes it self-sterile and a poor pollinator for other trees.

It is a medium to large green to greenish-yellow apple with creamy, crisp, very juicy flesh and a spicy-sweet, slightly sharp flavour that has more depth than its Golden Delicious parent. It holds its shape well when cooked, so it works for both fresh eating and baking, and it keeps for several months in cool storage. It is prone to blister spot.

paragraphs: Orleans Reinette is a French dessert apple first recorded around 1776 and named for the city of Orleans. Long valued across Europe as a high-quality variety, it received a Royal Horticultural Society Award of Merit in 1914 and again in 1921. DNA work points to Reinette de Hollande and Court Pendu Plat as parents.

The skin is golden yellow overlaid with bronze russet and a red-orange blush, giving a rough, sandy texture. The firm, fine-textured, creamy flesh has a rich flavour often described as orange and nuts, making it a connoisseur's eating apple at the end of the season.

paragraphs: Peasgood's Nonsuch was raised by Mrs Peasgood at Stamford, Lincolnshire, England, from seed sown about 1858. A basket of the fruit won first prize at the Burghley Park agricultural show, and the variety received a First Class Certificate from the Royal Horticultural Society in 1872, which helped launch it as a garden favourite.

It is a large, handsome culinary apple, pale yellow-green flushing to orange-yellow with red striping and some russet. The flesh is a little coarse and moderately juicy, cooking down well to a sweet, well-flavoured puree, though good specimens are pleasant enough to eat fresh.

paragraphs: Pink Lady is the trademark name for fruit of the Cripps Pink cultivar, which was bred in Western Australia. John Cripps, working at the state Department of Agriculture, crossed the Australian apple Lady Williams with the American Golden Delicious in 1973, aiming to combine the long storage life of one parent with the sweetness of the other. It became one of the first apples sold under a trademarked brand, with only fruit that meets a set quality standard sold as Pink Lady.

The fruit has crisp, dense flesh and a high balance of sugar and acid that gives it a sweet-tart, almost effervescent flavour, with a pink blush over greenish-yellow skin. It is one of the last apples picked each season and needs a long, warm growing period, so it performs better in warmer districts than on cold sites. The signature pink colour develops best where warm autumn days are followed by cool nights.

paragraphs: Pinkabelle is a compact, naturally dwarf form of Cripps Pink, the Western Australian apple marketed worldwide as Pink Lady. The point of difference is the tree, not the fruit: it is a true dwarf whose own growth is dwarfing rather than a standard variety grafted onto dwarfing rootstock, so it stays small (a couple of metres) while still bearing full sized apples. It is spur bearing and needs only minimal pruning, which makes it a popular pick for pots, courtyards and small gardens.

Eating quality follows its Pink Lady parentage: crisp, sweet, pinkish red fruit that colours up late in the season. Cripps Pink itself was bred by John Cripps in Western Australia from a cross of Lady Williams and Golden Delicious, and Pinkabelle is reported to have arisen from a Western Australian orchard. Being self sterile it fruits best with another apple flowering at the same time, and the dwarf Granny Smith Leprechaun is often paired with it.

Pinkabelle is a true dwarf whose tree is genetically dwarfing rather than grafted onto dwarfing rootstock, and it is spur bearing with full sized fruit.

paragraphs: Pixie Crunch, trialled as Co-op 33, is a modern disease-resistant apple from the cooperative breeding program of the Indiana, Illinois and New Jersey research stations, with the cross made in 1971 and selected at West Lafayette, Indiana. Golden Delicious and Rome both appear in its pedigree. It is genetically resistant to apple scab.

It is a small, deep-red dessert apple known above all for its exceptionally crisp, breaking texture, comparable to Honeycrisp. The flavour is sweet with a mild, slightly spicy character. It ripens around mid-September, a week or two after Gala, and hangs on the tree in good condition for several weeks.

paragraphs: Red Delicious began as a chance seedling found by Iowa farmer Jesse Hiatt, who first fruited it in the 1870s and named it Hawkeye after his home state. Stark Nurseries bought the rights, renamed it Stark Delicious, and from 1914 it became known as Red Delicious.

The original fruit was prized for its sweetness, but decades of selecting strains for deep red colour, storage life, and cosmetic appeal rather than taste left the modern apple widely criticised for blander flavour. It is a sweet, mild eating apple with thick skin and crisp flesh.

Bought by Stark Nurseries, renamed Stark Delicious, then Red Delicious from 1914

paragraphs: Red Fuji is a more deeply coloured bud sport of Fuji, the Japanese apple developed at the Tohoku Research Station from a cross of Red Delicious and Ralls Janet. Where standard Fuji often colours as a light blush or faint stripe, Red Fuji selections develop a bolder, richer red skin, while keeping the flavour and texture of the parent.
As with Fuji, the flesh is sweet, very crisp and dense, and the fruit is noted as an exceptionally good keeper that stores well. It is grown as a fresh-eating dessert apple.

Fuji was developed in Japan from a cross of Red Delicious and Ralls Janet.

paragraphs: Redlove is not a single apple but a series of red-fleshed cultivars bred by Swiss breeder Markus Kobelt of the Lubera nursery, with selections such as Calypso, Circe, Era and Odysso. They are the result of crossing sour red-fleshed apples with sweeter, scab-resistant varieties, a process that took many years of natural cross-pollination to combine red flesh with good eating quality and disease resistance.

The standout trait is flesh that is red right through, not just red-skinned, typically with a white line through the middle. The red colour is a natural (non-GM) trait traced back to red-fleshed wild apples. Because Redlove is a trademarked brand covering several distinct selections, flavour and season vary by cultivar.

paragraphs: Reine des Hâtives is a French cider apple from Normandy, grown by the nurseryman Dieppois of Yvetot in the Seine-Maritime and first fruiting in 1872. It is a sweet type rather than a sharp one, valued for the soft, sweet, amber juice it yields rather than for the table.

The fruit is medium to small, round-conic and often lopsided with distinct ribs, pale yellow flushed with an orange blush. The flesh is white, tender and medium-grained, moderately juicy, very sweet and aromatic with little acidity. It ripens early and the apples tend to drop as soon as they are ready, which suits its main job as a sweet cider variety, often used for blending.

paragraphs: Rome Beauty traces to a chance seedling found in 1817 by Joel Gillet in a nursery shipment, planted on the banks of the Ohio River in Rome Township, Ohio. Originally called Gillett's Seedling, it was renamed Rome Beauty in 1832 in honour of the township and went on to become a staple of American commerce.

The fruit is rounded, glossy and deep red with a thick skin and firm flesh. It is mainly a baking apple, holding its shape well when cooked while its flavour develops with heat. Fresh, its taste is milder and less sweet or tart than many dessert varieties, so it is favoured more for the kitchen than the fruit bowl.

Rounded, glossy, deep red with thick skin and firm flesh

paragraphs: Royal Gala is a more highly coloured sport of Gala, the New Zealand apple bred from Kidd's Orange Red crossed with Golden Delicious. The redder mutation was discovered by Bill ten Hove at Matamata, New Zealand, who noticed improved red striping on a single branch of a Gala tree. Apart from the deeper, more striped red skin, it keeps the parent Gala's eating character.

Like Gala, it is a sweet, crisp dessert apple eaten fresh. The name commemorates Queen Elizabeth II, who reportedly enjoyed Gala apples on a visit to New Zealand, and the redder sport has largely displaced the original paler Gala in commercial markets.

paragraphs: Somerset Redstreak is a traditional English cider apple from Somerset, dating from before 1900. It is a late, mild bittersweet variety with deep red stripes over green skin, yielding a sweet, astringent juice of vintage quality.
It remains one of the main commercial cider cultivars. The vigorous trees crop well but often biennially, and the fruit needs to be pressed promptly after harvest rather than stored.

Late, mild bittersweet variety with a sweet, astringent juice
Vintage quality cider apple and one of the main commercial cider cultivars

paragraphs: Spartan is a Canadian apple developed by R.C. Palmer at the federal research station in Summerland, British Columbia, and introduced in 1936. It is a McIntosh-type apple, long thought to be a McIntosh cross with Yellow Newtown Pippin, though later genetic analysis ruled out Newtown Pippin and left the second parent a mystery.

The fruit is deep red, sometimes almost purple-red, over a snowy-white flesh. It is crisp and juicy off the tree with a sweet, slightly wine-like (vinous) flavour, softening a little after picking. It is regarded as a good all-purpose apple for fresh eating and cooking.

paragraphs: Stoke Red is a bittersharp English cider apple from Somerset, named after the village of Rodney Stoke where it rose to prominence in the 1920s after dairy farmer Herbert Sealey entered it in the Long Ashton Research Station competition. The small fruit is red striped over yellow and ripens very late, usually November.

It is regarded as a vintage quality variety, high in both tannin and acid, often compared to Kingston Black. That tannin and acid balance lets it make a good single varietal cider on its own, and it is highly resistant to scab, though the tree has an unruly growth habit.

paragraphs: Sundowner is the trademark name for fruit of the Cripps Red cultivar, bred by John Cripps at the Stoneville Research Station in Western Australia in 1973 from a cross of Golden Delicious and Lady Williams. It shares its parentage with Cripps Pink, sold as Pink Lady, and is also marketed as Joya in Europe.

It is a red-blushed apple with distinctive white lenticels, and crisp, dense, very juicy flesh that balances good acidity with sweetness, giving a tangy yet sweet flavour. It ripens very late in the season and is a warm-climate, low-chill apple, which is why it grows well in places like Western Australia. It is mainly eaten fresh.

paragraphs: Sweet Alford is a traditional cider apple from Devon, England, dating to the 18th century. In the English four-category system for cider apples it sits at the sweet to mild bittersweet end, meaning it brings sweetness and only light tannin rather than the sharp acidity of sharps and bittersharps. It is regarded as a vintage variety, the term cidermakers use for apples that can make a good single-variety cider, though it is often blended with more tannic bittersweets to add balance.

The fruit is medium and conical with yellow, waxy skin flushed with orange, and the flesh is sweet and white with little astringency. As a pure sweet, its low acid and low tannin make it useful for softening over-sharp blends. It is a late-ripening variety.

paragraphs: Sweet Coppin is an old cider apple from Devon, England, that was once common in the cider orchards around Exeter. It is classed as a sweet, sometimes very mildly bittersweet, cider variety in the English system, producing a full, sweet juice with low acidity and low tannin. Cidermakers value it for adding sweetness and softening over-sharp blends, and it is rated a vintage variety capable of a pleasant cider on its own.
The fruit is a pale yellow apple with a pink blush and fine russeting, ripening mid to late season. It is held in the UK National Fruit Collection, where its accession number is 1989-112.

paragraphs: Tremlett's Bitter is a classic English bittersweet cider apple from Devon, dating to around 1880. The deep red, conical fruit is famous for its very robust, almost brutal tannins, earning it a reputation as a heavyweight bittersweet.

Because the bitterness is so pronounced, it is usually used in blends with sweeter or sharper varieties, where its structure and tannin add body. It tends to bear biennially, with heavy crops every second year.

paragraphs: Tropic Sweet (selection Fla. 90-3) is a low-chill apple from the University of Florida breeding program at Gainesville, selected in 1990 and patented in 1995. It traces back to a cross involving Anna, and it is bred specifically for warm subtropical climates where high-chill apples will not set fruit.

The fruit is sweeter, firmer, and less red than Anna, with low acidity giving a very sweet taste and a crisp texture often compared to Gala. It carries a partial red blush over a yellow-green skin and ripens early in the season. The trees are not self-fruitful, so they need a pollinator such as Anna or Dorsett Golden.

paragraphs: Tropical Beauty is a warm-climate dessert apple raised around 1930 by Meredith B. Strapp in Maidstone, South Africa, from a seedling of unknown parentage. It was named and introduced in Australia in 1958 by Longbecker Nurseries, and it is valued as a low-chill variety that fruits in subtropical conditions where most apples will not.

The fruit is medium sized and round-conic with ribbed, angular sides, showing a dark red wash over a greenish-yellow base. The flesh is yellowish white, firm and rather coarse, with a dry, sweet flavour. It is only partly self-fertile and crops best with a compatible pollinator nearby.

paragraphs: Twenty Ounce is an American heritage apple believed to come from Cayuga County, New York, discovered as a chance seedling in the early 19th century and first exhibited in 1843. Its parentage is unknown. The name reflects its remarkable size, with fruit that can grow very large and heavy.

It is a large apple with a yellow-green base overlaid by red blush, striations and striping, and firm, dense, pale flesh. The flavour is mildly tart, and it was prized for generations as a premier cooking apple, especially for pies and sauce. Today it is rare, kept going mainly by heirloom growers.

paragraphs: Tydeman's Early Worcester is an early-season dessert apple bred at the East Malling Research Station in Kent. It came from a cross of Worcester Pearmain and McIntosh made in 1929 by the apple breeder H.M. Tydeman, and was introduced in 1945. The variety takes after Worcester Pearmain but gains extra sweetness and juiciness from its McIntosh parent.

The fruit ripens early, with a pale yellow skin heavily flushed and striped in crimson red. The flavour is rich, sweet with a little acidity. It is a tip bearer, which makes it less suited to training as a cordon or espalier.

paragraphs: Verité is an old French cider apple, far less familiar in the English speaking cider world than the famous Norman bittersweets. Its juice runs to the sharp side, with an intense, brisk and notably fruity character rather than the soft tannin of a true bittersweet, which is why Australian references recommend it as a blending component, bringing acidity and lift to a cider, rather than as a single varietal.

The fruit itself is small and roundish, green washed with a dark maroon flush on the sunny side, with crisp, firm, greenish white flesh. It is harvested late in the cider season and is noted for keeping well in storage after picking.

paragraphs: Vista Bella is one of the earliest apples of the season, raised at Rutgers University in New Jersey in the mid 1950s and introduced in 1956. It is widely regarded as among the first red apples to ripen, maturing in mid summer well before most other varieties.

The fruit is medium-sized with light yellow-green skin that flushes deep red where the sun reaches it, often deepening to a solid crimson when fully ripe. As an early apple it is best eaten soon after picking rather than stored. Its early timing makes it useful for gardeners wanting fresh fruit at the very start of the apple season.

paragraphs: Wandin Pride is an Australian apple from Victoria, also known as Wandin Glory, recorded as a Scoresby cross of Emperor Alexander and Jonathan. Its defining feature is a naturally weeping, pendulous growth habit, unusual among apples, which gives it ornamental value as well as fruit. Growers often train the drooping laterals along a trellis or pergola.

It bears well and is prized for flavour, producing small to medium red-blushed fruit that many describe as close to a Jonathan in taste, good both for fresh eating and in desserts. It is self-sterile, so it needs another apple flowering nearby to set a crop.

paragraphs: Worcester Pearmain is an early-season English apple, raised in the St John's district of Worcester in the 1870s and introduced to market in 1874. It received a Royal Horticultural Society First Class Certificate in 1875 and was for many years the most popular early-autumn apple in England.

Its claim to fame is a distinctive strawberry-like flavour, though the intensity varies with the season and ripeness. The fruit is sweet rather than sharp, with a bright red flush. Its parentage is unknown, but it is widely thought to be a seedling of Devonshire Quarrenden, another early apple noted for the same strawberry note. As a summer apple it is best eaten soon after picking.

paragraphs: Yarlington Mill is a classic Somerset bittersweet, named for the spot where it was found, a chance wild seedling growing beside the mill race in the village of Yarlington, North Cadbury. From that single wilding it went on to be planted across Somerset, Devon and the wider West Country, becoming a staple of twentieth century English cider orchards.

The juice is a mild, full bittersweet with soft, rounded tannin, good enough to stand alone as a single varietal cider as well as anchoring blends, and Australian references have called it possibly the doyenne of cider apples. The fruit is small to medium and flushed red, ripening mid season, and the tree crops heavily but with a strong biennial streak.
